resource.title: Basic Electrical Engineering — Top 400 Interview Q&A

questions.question: What is Electricity?
questions.answer: Flow of electric charge (electrons or ions) in a conductor due to a potential difference (voltage).

questions.question: What is Electric Current?
questions.answer: The rate of flow of electric charge.

questions.question: What is Voltage?
questions.answer: Work done per unit charge to move charge between two points.

questions.question: What is Resistance?
questions.answer: Opposition offered by a material to current flow.

questions.question: Define Ohm's Law.
questions.answer: Current is directly proportional to applied voltage and inversely proportional to resistance.

questions.question: What happens to current if resistance increases (at constant voltage)?
questions.answer: Current decreases (since I = V/R).

questions.question: What is Power in Electrical Circuits?
questions.answer: Power = Voltage × Current.

questions.question: What is Electrical Energy?
questions.answer: Energy consumed over time.

questions.question: Difference between AC and DC?
questions.answer: AC: current changes direction periodically (e.g., 50 Hz). DC: current flows one direction only (e.g., battery).

questions.question: What is Frequency?
questions.answer: Number of cycles per second in AC.

questions.question: What is Conductance?
questions.answer: Reciprocal of resistance.

questions.question: Define Electrical Power Factor.
questions.answer: PF = cos φ = Real Power / Apparent Power. Range 0 to 1.

questions.question: Why is Power Factor important?
questions.answer: Higher PF means less losses and better efficiency in power systems.

questions.question: What are Passive Components?
questions.answer: Components that do not generate energy (Resistor, Capacitor, Inductor).

questions.question: What are Active Components?
questions.answer: Components that provide energy (Transistors, Diodes, Vacuum tubes).

questions.question: What is Short Circuit?
questions.answer: When live conductor directly touches neutral/earth with very low resistance path → very high current.

questions.question: What is Open Circuit?
questions.answer: When circuit path is broken → no current flows.

questions.question: What is Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)?
questions.answer: Sum of currents entering a node = Sum of currents leaving the node.

questions.question: What is Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)?
questions.answer: Algebraic sum of voltages around a closed loop = 0.

questions.question: What is a Fuse?
questions.answer: Protective device that melts when current exceeds rated value.

questions.question: Difference between Fuse and MCB?
questions.answer: Fuse: one-time protection, must be replaced. MCB: resettable protective device.

questions.question: What is Earthing?
questions.answer: Connecting metallic parts of equipment to ground to prevent shock.

questions.question: Types of Earthing?
questions.answer: 1) Plate Earthing  2) Pipe Earthing  3) Rod Earthing  4) Strip Earthing

questions.question: What is Electric Shock?
questions.answer: When current passes through human body due to potential difference between body and ground.

questions.question: Safe limit of current for humans?
questions.answer: 30 mA (beyond this may cause death).

questions.question: What is a Transformer?
questions.answer: Static device that transfers AC power from one circuit to another at same frequency but different voltage.

questions.question: Types of Transformer?
questions.answer: Step-up, Step-down, Isolation.

questions.question: Transformer Losses?
questions.answer: Core loss (hysteresis + eddy current) and Copper loss.

questions.question: What is a DC Generator?
questions.answer: Machine that converts mechanical energy into DC electrical energy.

questions.question: Types of DC Generator?
questions.answer: Separately excited and Self-excited (Shunt, Series, Compound).

questions.question: What is a DC Motor?
questions.answer: Machine that converts DC electrical energy into mechanical energy.

questions.question: Types of DC Motor?
questions.answer: Shunt, Series, Compound.

questions.question: Applications of DC Shunt Motor?
questions.answer: Fans, lathes, machine tools (constant speed).

questions.question: Applications of DC Series Motor?
questions.answer: Electric traction, cranes, elevators (high starting torque).

questions.question: What is Induction Motor?
questions.answer: AC motor where rotor receives power by induction from stator field.

questions.question: Types of Induction Motor?
questions.answer: Squirrel Cage IM, Slip Ring IM.

questions.question: What is Synchronous Motor?
questions.answer: AC motor running at synchronous speed.

questions.question: Why Induction Motor is called Asynchronous?
questions.answer: Because rotor speed is less than synchronous speed.

questions.question: What is Alternator?
questions.answer: AC generator that converts mechanical energy to AC electrical energy.

questions.question: Voltage Regulation of Alternator?
questions.answer: % change in voltage from no-load to full-load at constant speed.

questions.question: Synchronization of Alternators?
questions.answer: Process of connecting alternator to busbar with same voltage, frequency, and phase.

questions.question: What is Stepper Motor?
questions.answer: Motor that rotates in steps when pulses are applied.

questions.question: What is Universal Motor?
questions.answer: Motor that runs on both AC and DC supply (mixers, drills).

questions.question: What is Single Phase Motor?
questions.answer: Motor working on 1-phase AC supply (ceiling fan).

questions.question: What is 3-Phase Motor?
questions.answer: Motor working on 3-phase AC supply (industrial motors).

questions.question: What is Back EMF in Motor?
questions.answer: EMF induced in motor winding which opposes applied voltage.

questions.question: What is Power System?
questions.answer: Network for generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity.

questions.question: Types of Power Plants?
questions.answer: Thermal, Hydro, Nuclear, Wind, Solar.

questions.question: What is Transmission Line?
questions.answer: High voltage lines that transmit power from generating station to substations.

questions.question: What is Distribution System?
questions.answer: Low/medium voltage system that supplies power to consumers.

questions.question: Types of Transmission Lines?
questions.answer: Short (<80 km), Medium (80–250 km), Long (>250 km).

questions.question: What is Corona Effect?
questions.answer: Ionization of air around conductors due to high voltage.

questions.question: What are Insulators?
questions.answer: Materials that prevent leakage of current (porcelain, glass, polymer).

questions.question: What is Circuit Breaker?
questions.answer: Device to interrupt fault current automatically.

questions.question: Types of Circuit Breakers?
questions.answer: Oil CB, Air CB, SF6 CB, Vacuum CB.

questions.question: What is Relay?
questions.answer: Protective device that detects fault and sends signal to breaker.

questions.question: Types of Relays?
questions.answer: Overcurrent, Differential, Distance, Earth Fault.

questions.question: What is Load Curve?
questions.answer: Graph of load vs. time in power system.

questions.question: What is Load Factor?
questions.answer: Ratio of average load to maximum demand.

questions.question: What is Diversity Factor?
questions.answer: Sum of individual max demands / Group max demand.

questions.question: What is Plant Load Factor (PLF)?
questions.answer: Actual output / Maximum possible output.

questions.question: What is Tariff in Electricity?
questions.answer: Rate at which electrical energy is supplied to consumers.

questions.question: What is Demand Factor?
questions.answer: Max demand / Connected load.

questions.question: What is Reliability of Power System?
questions.answer: Ability to supply uninterrupted power.

questions.question: What is SCADA?
questions.answer: Supervisory Control And Data Acquisition (used in automation & monitoring).

questions.question: What is HVDC Transmission?
questions.answer: High Voltage Direct Current transmission, used for long-distance power transfer.

questions.question: Advantages of HVDC?
questions.answer: Less losses, long distance transmission, asynchronous grid interconnection.

questions.question: What is FACTS in Power System?
questions.answer: Flexible AC Transmission System — power electronics devices to improve stability.

questions.question: What is Smart Grid?
questions.answer: Modernized electrical grid with automation, two-way communication, and digital meters.

questions.question: What is Blackout?
questions.answer: Complete failure of power supply in a large area.

questions.question: What is Brownout?
questions.answer: Partial reduction in voltage supply for a period of time.

questions.question: What is an Electrical Measuring Instrument?
questions.answer: Device used to measure electrical quantities like current, voltage, power, energy, resistance, etc.

questions.question: Difference between Analog and Digital Instruments?
questions.answer: Analog: pointer-type display (ammeter, voltmeter). Digital: numeric display (digital multimeter).

questions.question: What is a Galvanometer?
questions.answer: Sensitive instrument that detects very small currents.

questions.question: What is an Ammeter?
questions.answer: Instrument used to measure electric current. Always connected in series.

questions.question: What is a Voltmeter?
questions.answer: Instrument used to measure voltage. Always connected in parallel.

questions.question: What is a Wattmeter?
questions.answer: Instrument to measure active power in AC/DC circuits.

questions.question: What is an Energy Meter (kWh meter)?
questions.answer: Instrument that measures electrical energy consumed by load in kilowatt-hours.

questions.question: Difference between 1-phase and 3-phase Energy Meter?
questions.answer: 1-phase: domestic, single coil. 3-phase: industries, measures 3-phase power.

questions.question: What is a Megger?
questions.answer: Instrument used to measure insulation resistance.

questions.question: What is a Multimeter?
questions.answer: Multipurpose instrument for measuring V, I, R (AC & DC).

questions.question: What is a CRO (Cathode Ray Oscilloscope)?
questions.answer: Device used to display waveform of electrical signals.

questions.question: What is a Clamp Meter?
questions.answer: Instrument used to measure current without breaking the circuit (clamps around conductor).

questions.question: Function of Potential Transformer (PT)?
questions.answer: Steps down high voltage to low for measurement and protection.

questions.question: Function of Current Transformer (CT)?
questions.answer: Steps down high current to safe value for measurement and relays.

questions.question: Why CT secondary should not be kept open?
questions.answer: If open, high voltage is induced → dangerous to operator and CT.

questions.question: Why PT secondary should not be short-circuited?
questions.answer: Because it may damage PT winding (acts like a shorted transformer).

questions.question: What is Calibration of Instruments?
questions.answer: Process of adjusting instrument to give accurate reading compared to standard.

questions.question: What is Accuracy of Instruments?
questions.answer: Closeness of measured value to actual true value.

questions.question: What is Precision of Instruments?
questions.answer: Ability of instrument to reproduce same reading under same conditions.

questions.question: What is Sensitivity of Instruments?
questions.answer: Ratio of change in output reading per unit change in input quantity.

questions.question: What is Range of an Instrument?
questions.answer: Minimum to maximum value that instrument can measure.

questions.question: Difference between Absolute and Secondary Instruments?
questions.answer: Absolute: measures directly (e.g., tangent galvanometer). Secondary: needs calibration with absolute instruments.

questions.question: Difference between Indicating, Recording, and Integrating Instruments?
questions.answer: Indicating: instantaneous values (ammeter). Recording: continuous values (CRO). Integrating: total energy (kWh meter).

questions.question: Why Shunt is used in Ammeter?
questions.answer: To bypass extra current and extend range of ammeter.

questions.question: Why Multiplier is used in Voltmeter?
questions.answer: To limit current through meter movement and extend voltage range.

questions.question: What are Conducting Materials?
questions.answer: Materials that allow flow of electric current (copper, aluminium, silver).

questions.question: Why is Copper preferred over Aluminium in conductors?
questions.answer: High conductivity, low resistance, good mechanical strength.

questions.question: Why is Aluminium used in Transmission Lines?
questions.answer: Light weight, economical, corrosion resistant.

questions.question: What are Insulating Materials?
questions.answer: Materials that resist current flow (rubber, PVC, porcelain, mica).

questions.question: Properties of Good Insulating Material?
questions.answer: High resistivity, high dielectric strength, mechanical strength, heat resistance.

questions.question: What are Magnetic Materials?
questions.answer: Materials influenced by magnetic field (iron, nickel, cobalt).

questions.question: Types of Magnetic Materials?
questions.answer: Diamagnetic, Paramagnetic, Ferromagnetic.

questions.question: What is Hysteresis Loss?
questions.answer: Energy lost due to repeated magnetization and demagnetization in core.

questions.question: What is Eddy Current Loss?
questions.answer: Loss due to circulating currents induced in core.

questions.question: How is Eddy Current Loss reduced?
questions.answer: By using laminated core.

questions.question: What is Dielectric Material?
questions.answer: An insulating material that can store electrical energy (used in capacitors).

questions.question: What is Semiconductor?
questions.answer: Material whose conductivity lies between conductor and insulator (silicon, germanium).

questions.question: What is Superconductor?
questions.answer: Material with zero resistance below critical temperature.

questions.question: Applications of Superconductors?
questions.answer: Magnetic levitation trains, MRI machines, superconductor cables.

questions.question: What is Resistivity?
questions.answer: Property of material resisting current flow.

questions.question: What is Conductivity?
questions.answer: Reciprocal of resistivity.

questions.question: Why Silver is best conductor but not used widely?
questions.answer: Very costly. Copper is more economical.

questions.question: Why Tungsten is used in filament lamps?
questions.answer: High melting point, withstands high temperature.

questions.question: Why Silicon Steel is used in Transformer Cores?
questions.answer: Low hysteresis loss and high permeability.

questions.question: Why Aluminium is preferred for Busbars?
questions.answer: Lightweight, cheaper, good conductivity.

questions.question: What is Piezoelectric Material?
questions.answer: Material that generates voltage when mechanical stress is applied (quartz).

questions.question: What is Thermoelectric Effect?
questions.answer: Conversion of heat into electricity using thermocouples.

questions.question: What is Photoelectric Effect?
questions.answer: Emission of electrons from a material when exposed to light (used in solar cells).

questions.question: What is Ferrite Material?
questions.answer: Magnetic ceramic material used in transformers, inductors, and antennas.

questions.question: What is Carbon used for in electrical engineering?
questions.answer: Used in brushes of machines, resistors, arc lamps.

questions.question: What is a Diode?
questions.answer: Semiconductor device that allows current in one direction only.

questions.question: What is Forward Biasing in Diode?
questions.answer: When P-side is connected to +ve and N-side to −ve → current flows.

questions.question: What is Reverse Biasing in Diode?
questions.answer: When P-side is connected to −ve and N-side to +ve → no current flows.

questions.question: What is a Zener Diode?
questions.answer: Special diode designed to operate in reverse breakdown region → used for voltage regulation.

questions.question: What is a Transistor?
questions.answer: Semiconductor device used for amplification and switching.

questions.question: Types of Transistors?
questions.answer: NPN and PNP.

questions.question: What is SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier)?
questions.answer: A 4-layer semiconductor device used in power electronics for rectification and control.

questions.question: What is a Triac?
questions.answer: A bidirectional thyristor used for AC control.

questions.question: What is a Capacitor?
questions.answer: Device used to store electrical energy in electric field.

questions.question: Applications of Capacitor?
questions.answer: Power factor improvement, energy storage, filtering in rectifiers.

questions.question: What is Inductor?
questions.answer: Device used to store energy in magnetic field.

questions.question: Applications of Inductor?
questions.answer: Filters, transformers, energy storage.

questions.question: What is a Rectifier?
questions.answer: Device that converts AC to DC.

questions.question: Types of Rectifier?
questions.answer: Half-wave, Full-wave, Bridge rectifier.

questions.question: What is Inverter?
questions.answer: Device that converts DC to AC.

questions.question: What is UPS (Uninterrupted Power Supply)?
questions.answer: Device that provides backup power during mains failure.

questions.question: What is SMPS (Switch Mode Power Supply)?
questions.answer: Power supply that converts electrical power efficiently using high-frequency switching.

questions.question: What is Resonance in RLC Circuit?
questions.answer: Condition when inductive reactance = capacitive reactance → power factor = 1.

questions.question: What is Filter in Electronics?
questions.answer: Device that passes certain frequency and blocks others.

questions.question: Types of Filters?
questions.answer: Low Pass, High Pass, Band Pass, Band Stop.

questions.question: What is Oscillator?
questions.answer: Circuit that generates continuous AC waveform without input.

questions.question: Difference between Amplifier and Oscillator?
questions.answer: Amplifier: increases strength of input signal. Oscillator: produces continuous signal without external input.

questions.question: What is Superposition Theorem?
questions.answer: In a linear circuit with multiple sources, total current/voltage is the algebraic sum of effects of each source acting alone.

questions.question: What is Thevenin's Theorem?
questions.answer: Any two-terminal linear circuit can be replaced by an equivalent voltage source (Vth) in series with resistance (Rth).

questions.question: What is Norton's Theorem?
questions.answer: Any two-terminal linear circuit can be replaced by an equivalent current source (In) in parallel with resistance (Rn).

questions.question: What is Reciprocity Theorem?
questions.answer: In a linear, bilateral circuit, if voltage is applied at one point and current measured at another, interchanging them gives the same result.

questions.question: What is Maximum Power Transfer Theorem?
questions.answer: Maximum power is transferred to load when Load Resistance = Source Resistance (RL = RS).

questions.question: What is Millman's Theorem?
questions.answer: Parallel voltage sources with resistances can be replaced by a single equivalent voltage source.

questions.question: What is Tellegen's Theorem?
questions.answer: Algebraic sum of power in a network is zero at all times.

questions.question: What is Supermesh Analysis?
questions.answer: Technique in mesh analysis when a current source lies between two meshes.

questions.question: What is Supernode Analysis?
questions.answer: Technique in nodal analysis when a voltage source lies between two nodes.

questions.question: What is Duality in Networks?
questions.answer: Relationship between two networks with interchanged quantities (Voltage↔Current, Series↔Parallel).

questions.question: What is Two-Port Network?
questions.answer: A network with two input and two output terminals.

questions.question: Parameters of Two-Port Network?
questions.answer: Z-parameters, Y-parameters, h-parameters, Transmission parameters.

questions.question: Balanced Condition in Wheatstone Bridge?
questions.answer: When ratio of resistances in two arms is equal, no current flows through galvanometer.

questions.question: Applications of Wheatstone Bridge?
questions.answer: Measurement of resistance.

questions.question: What is Delta-Star (Δ-Y) Transformation?
questions.answer: Method to simplify 3-phase circuit analysis by converting delta to star network.

questions.question: What is Star-Delta (Y-Δ) Transformation?
questions.answer: Method to convert star connected network to delta for simplification.

questions.question: Time Constant of RL Circuit?
questions.formula: τ = L / R (time for current to reach 63% of final value)

questions.question: What is Transient Response?
questions.answer: Short time behavior of circuit before reaching steady state.

questions.question: What is Steady-State Response?
questions.answer: Long-term behavior of circuit after transients die out.

questions.question: Resonance in Series RLC Circuit?
questions.answer: When XL = XC → impedance minimum → current maximum.

questions.question: Resonance in Parallel RLC Circuit?
questions.answer: When XL = XC → impedance maximum → current minimum.

questions.question: What is Bandwidth of Resonance?
questions.answer: Range of frequencies where circuit response is significant. BW = f2 − f1.

questions.question: What is Selectivity?
questions.answer: Ability of resonant circuit to select a narrow band of frequencies.

questions.question: What is RMS Value?
questions.answer: Square root of mean of squares of instantaneous values.

questions.question: Average Value of AC?
questions.answer: Average of instantaneous values over half cycle.

questions.question: What is Form Factor?
questions.answer: Ratio of RMS value to Average value. For sine wave: 1.11.

questions.question: What is Peak Factor (Crest Factor)?
questions.answer: Ratio of maximum value to RMS value. For sine wave: 1.414.

questions.question: What is Phase in AC?
questions.answer: Relative position of waveform compared to reference.

questions.question: What is Phase Difference?
questions.answer: Angular difference between two waveforms.

questions.question: What is Lagging Power Factor?
questions.answer: When current lags voltage (inductive load).

questions.question: What is Leading Power Factor?
questions.answer: When current leads voltage (capacitive load).

questions.question: What is Active Power?
questions.answer: Real power consumed in circuit.

questions.question: What is Reactive Power?
questions.answer: Power stored & released in inductors/capacitors.

questions.question: What is Apparent Power?
questions.answer: Combination of active & reactive power.

questions.question: What is Power Triangle?
questions.answer: Graphical representation of Active, Reactive, and Apparent power.

questions.question: What is Harmonic in AC System?
questions.answer: Voltages or currents with frequencies multiple of fundamental frequency.

questions.question: What is THD (Total Harmonic Distortion)?
questions.answer: Ratio of RMS of harmonics to RMS of fundamental.

questions.question: What is Skin Effect?
questions.answer: At high frequency, current concentrates near conductor surface.

questions.question: What is Proximity Effect?
questions.answer: Non-uniform distribution of current due to nearby conductors.

questions.question: What is Ferranti Effect?
questions.answer: Voltage rise at receiving end of long transmission line under light load.

questions.question: What is Corona Discharge?
questions.answer: Ionization of air around conductor due to high electric stress.

questions.question: What are Symmetrical Components?
questions.answer: Any unbalanced 3-phase system can be resolved into: Positive, Negative, and Zero sequence components.

questions.question: What is Unbalanced Load?
questions.answer: When load impedances in 3-phase are not equal.

questions.question: What is Balanced Load?
questions.answer: When load impedances in 3-phase are equal.

questions.question: What is Neutral Point in 3-phase System?
questions.answer: Common junction point in star connected system.

questions.question: Why Neutral Wire is needed?
questions.answer: To carry unbalanced current in star connection.

questions.question: What is Phase Sequence?
questions.answer: Order in which phases attain maximum positive value (R → Y → B).

questions.question: What happens if Phase Sequence is changed?
questions.answer: In 3-phase motors, rotation direction reverses.

questions.question: What is Power Electronics?
questions.answer: Branch of electronics dealing with control & conversion of electric power using semiconductor devices.

questions.question: What is Rectifier?
questions.answer: Converts AC to DC.

questions.question: What is Controlled Rectifier?
questions.answer: Uses SCRs for variable DC output.

questions.question: What is Cycloconverter?
questions.answer: Converts AC of one frequency to AC of another frequency.

questions.question: What is Chopper?
questions.answer: Converts fixed DC to variable DC.

questions.question: What is Inverter?
questions.answer: Converts DC to AC.

questions.question: What is PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)?
questions.answer: Technique of controlling output voltage of inverter/chopper by varying pulse width.

questions.question: What is VSI (Voltage Source Inverter)?
questions.answer: Inverter where input is constant DC voltage source.

questions.question: What is CSI (Current Source Inverter)?
questions.answer: Inverter where input is constant DC current source.

questions.question: What is Commutation in SCR?
questions.answer: Process of turning off SCR.

questions.question: Types of Commutation in SCR?
questions.answer: Natural Commutation and Forced Commutation.

questions.question: What is Gate Triggering of SCR?
questions.answer: Applying gate current to turn on SCR.

questions.question: What is a DIAC?
questions.answer: Two-terminal device used to trigger TRIAC.

questions.question: What is a TRIAC?
questions.answer: AC switch that can conduct in both directions.

questions.question: What is IGBT (Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor)?
questions.answer: Power semiconductor device combining MOSFET input with BJT output.

questions.question: Applications of IGBT?
questions.answer: Inverters, UPS, electric vehicles.

questions.question: What is V/f Control in Induction Motor?
questions.answer: Speed control method where voltage-to-frequency ratio is kept constant.

questions.question: What is PID Controller?
questions.answer: Control system device using Proportional, Integral, and Derivative control.

questions.question: What is PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)?
questions.answer: Industrial control device used for automation.
questions.aye_note: Core specialty of AYE TechEngine — covered in depth in PLC training modules.

questions.question: What is SCADA in Power Systems?
questions.answer: Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition — monitoring & control of substations.

questions.question: What is FACTS Technology?
questions.answer: Flexible AC Transmission System using power electronics for stability improvement.

questions.question: What is STATCOM?
questions.answer: Static Synchronous Compensator used for reactive power compensation.

questions.question: What is SVC (Static VAR Compensator)?
questions.answer: Power electronics device to control reactive power in transmission.

questions.question: What is HVDC Converter Station?
questions.answer: Station used to convert AC ↔ DC for HVDC transmission.

questions.question: What is Smart Grid?
questions.answer: Advanced power grid with digital communication, automation & renewable integration.

questions.question: What is a Fuse? (detailed)
questions.answer: Protective device that melts when excessive current flows. Prevents damage to equipment by breaking the circuit. Made of low melting point material like tin, lead, or silver.

questions.question: Disadvantages of a Fuse?
questions.answer: Once blown it must be replaced; not suitable for overload protection (only short-circuit); cannot be used for frequent switching.

questions.question: What is a Circuit Breaker?
questions.answer: Switch that automatically interrupts fault current. Can be reset and reused. Types: OCB, ACB, VCB, SF6 CB, MCB.

questions.question: Difference between Fuse and Circuit Breaker?
questions.answer: Fuse: single-use, melts. CB: reusable, mechanically/electrically operated. Fuse reacts faster, but CB is more reliable.

questions.question: What is MCB?
questions.answer: Miniature Circuit Breaker. Used for low voltage domestic supply. Trips automatically during overload or short-circuit.

questions.question: What is RCCB?
questions.answer: Residual Current Circuit Breaker. Protects humans from electric shock due to leakage current. Trips when neutral & phase currents are unequal.

questions.question: What is ELCB?
questions.answer: Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker. Detects earth leakage current and disconnects supply.

questions.question: Difference between RCCB and ELCB?
questions.answer: RCCB: detects imbalance between phase & neutral currents. ELCB: detects earth leakage voltage. RCCB is modern & more accurate.

questions.question: What is SF6 Circuit Breaker?
questions.answer: Uses sulphur hexafluoride gas for arc quenching. High efficiency, used in EHV substations. Non-flammable and excellent dielectric.

questions.question: What is an Air Circuit Breaker (ACB)?
questions.answer: Uses air as arc quenching medium. Used in low/medium voltage applications.

questions.question: What is a Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)?
questions.answer: Uses vacuum for arc extinction. No gas required, very reliable. Mostly used for 11 kV to 33 kV systems.

questions.question: What is an Oil Circuit Breaker (OCB)?
questions.answer: Uses transformer oil for arc extinction. Oil also works as insulation.

questions.question: What is a Lightning Arrester?
questions.answer: Device used to protect equipment from lightning surges. Diverts surge to earth safely.

questions.question: What is Earthing? (detailed)
questions.answer: Process of connecting electrical installation to earth. Protects from electric shock & equipment damage.

questions.question: Types of Earthing? (detailed)
questions.answer: Plate Earthing, Pipe Earthing, Rod Earthing, Strip Earthing.

questions.question: Typical earth resistance value?
questions.answer: Residential: < 5 Ω. Substations: < 1 Ω.

questions.question: Why is Neutral connected to Earth?
questions.answer: To maintain voltage stability and provide return path for fault current.

questions.question: What is a Transformer? (detailed)
questions.answer: Static device that transfers AC power from one circuit to another at constant frequency. Works on Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.

questions.question: Types of Transformer? (detailed)
questions.answer: Step-up, Step-down, Power Transformer, Distribution Transformer, Instrument Transformer.

questions.question: Step-up Transformer?
questions.answer: Converts low voltage → high voltage. Used in power plants for transmission.

questions.question: Step-down Transformer?
questions.answer: Converts high voltage → low voltage. Used in distribution systems.

questions.question: Power Transformer?
questions.answer: Used in transmission network. Works at full load for 24×7. High efficiency (99%).

questions.question: Distribution Transformer?
questions.answer: Steps down voltage near consumers. Works at light load most of the time. Designed for good voltage regulation.

questions.question: Instrument Transformer?
questions.answer: Used for measurement & protection. Types: CT (Current Transformer), PT (Potential Transformer).

questions.question: Current Transformer (CT)?
questions.answer: Reduces high current → small measurable current. Secondary usually 5 A or 1 A.

questions.question: Potential Transformer (PT)?
questions.answer: Reduces high voltage → small measurable voltage. Secondary usually 110 V.

questions.question: Why are Instrument Transformers used?
questions.answer: For safe measurement of high current/voltage and to isolate measuring instruments from HV side.

questions.question: What is an Autotransformer?
questions.answer: Single winding transformer with tapping. Smaller, cheaper, but no isolation between primary & secondary.

questions.question: What is an Isolation Transformer?
questions.answer: Provides electrical isolation between primary & secondary. Used for safety in medical & electronic equipment.

questions.question: EMF equation of Transformer?
questions.formula: E = 4.44 f N Φmax (f = frequency, N = turns, Φmax = max flux)

questions.question: Core Loss in Transformer?
questions.answer: Loss in iron core due to hysteresis + eddy current. Constant with load.

questions.question: Copper Loss in Transformer?
questions.answer: Loss due to resistance of winding (I²R loss). Variable with load.

questions.question: All-day Efficiency?
questions.answer: Ratio of kWh output / kWh input in 24 hours. Important for distribution transformers.

questions.question: No-load Test of Transformer?
questions.answer: Performed to measure core loss. Primary connected to supply, secondary open.

questions.question: Short-circuit Test of Transformer?
questions.answer: Performed to measure copper loss. Secondary shorted, small voltage applied on primary.

questions.question: Parallel Operation of Transformer?
questions.answer: Connecting two or more transformers to share load. Conditions: same polarity, voltage ratio, phase sequence, % impedance.

questions.question: Buchholz Relay?
questions.answer: Gas-operated protection relay in oil-filled transformers. Detects internal faults (like winding failure).

questions.question: Tap Changer in Transformer?
questions.answer: Used to adjust turns ratio → control secondary voltage. Types: On-load tap changer (OLTC), Off-load tap changer.

questions.question: Harmonic in Transformer?
questions.answer: Distortion in waveform due to non-linear loads. Causes extra losses, noise, heating.

questions.question: Inrush Current in Transformer?
questions.answer: High magnetizing current at switching ON. Can damage transformer if not controlled.

questions.question: Polarity of Transformer?
questions.answer: Determines relative direction of induced EMFs in primary & secondary. Tested by polarity test.

questions.question: Load Sharing in Parallel Transformers?
questions.answer: Transformers must share load according to their kVA ratings.

questions.question: Ferranti Effect?
questions.answer: In long transmission lines, receiving end voltage > sending end voltage during light load due to charging current.

questions.question: Corona Effect?
questions.answer: Ionization of air around conductors at high voltage. Produces hissing sound, light, ozone.

questions.question: How to reduce Corona?
questions.answer: Increase conductor size; increase spacing; use bundled conductors.

questions.question: Skin Effect?
questions.answer: AC current tends to concentrate on surface of conductor. Effective resistance increases.

questions.question: How to reduce Skin Effect?
questions.answer: Use stranded conductors (like Litz wire); reduce frequency.

questions.question: Proximity Effect?
questions.answer: Non-uniform distribution of current due to magnetic fields of nearby conductors.

questions.question: Dielectric Loss?
questions.answer: Loss in insulating material when it is subjected to AC voltage.

questions.question: Sag in Transmission Line?
questions.answer: Vertical distance between conductor and straight line joining two supports. Important for mechanical design.

questions.question: Factors affecting Sag?
questions.answer: Conductor weight, span length, tension in conductor, temperature.

questions.question: String Efficiency in Insulators?
questions.formula: %Efficiency = (Voltage across string / (n × Average unit voltage)) × 100. Ideally 100%, but less due to non-uniform distribution.

questions.question: Methods to improve String Efficiency?
questions.answer: Use grading rings; use capacitive grading.

questions.question: What is a Circuit?
questions.answer: Closed conducting path through which current flows.

questions.question: What is an Open Circuit?
questions.answer: Circuit where the path is broken → no current flows.

questions.question: What is a Short Circuit?
questions.answer: Direct connection between two points of different potential. Causes excessive current flow.

questions.question: What is Overload?
questions.answer: Condition when current > rated current of equipment.

questions.question: What is a Relay?
questions.answer: Protective device which senses fault & operates circuit breaker.

questions.question: Types of Relays?
questions.answer: Electromagnetic, Induction, Distance, Differential, Overcurrent, Buchholz.

questions.question: Differential Relay?
questions.answer: Compares current at two points of equipment. Trips if difference > limit.

questions.question: Overcurrent Relay?
questions.answer: Operates when current > set value. Used for feeders.

questions.question: Distance Relay?
questions.answer: Operates based on impedance measurement between relay & fault point. Used in transmission line protection.

questions.question: Induction Relay?
questions.answer: Works on principle of induction motor. Used in AC circuits.

questions.question: Time Multiplier Setting (TMS)?
questions.answer: Multiplier that adjusts operating time of relay.

questions.question: IDMT Relay?
questions.answer: Inverse Definite Minimum Time Relay. Time of operation inversely proportional to fault current.

questions.question: Protection Coordination?
questions.answer: Ensuring that the nearest protective device operates first during a fault.

questions.question: CT Saturation?
questions.answer: When CT core is over-magnetized and output is distorted.

questions.question: Burden of CT/PT?
questions.answer: Load connected to secondary of CT/PT (in VA).

questions.question: What is an Exciter?
questions.answer: Device used to supply DC excitation to synchronous machine field winding.

questions.question: Types of Exciter?
questions.answer: DC Exciter, AC Exciter with rectifier, Static Exciter.

questions.question: Armature Reaction?
questions.answer: Effect of armature flux on main field flux in a machine. Causes distortion of main flux.

questions.question: How to reduce Armature Reaction?
questions.answer: Use interpoles; use compensating winding.

questions.question: Load Regulation (Power Supply)?
questions.formula: %Load Reg = ((Vnl − Vfl) / Vfl) × 100. Lower % means better regulation.

questions.question: Line Regulation?
questions.formula: %Line Reg = (ΔVout / ΔVin) × 100. Good SMPS maintains output for wide input (e.g., 90–270 V AC).

questions.question: Voltage Stabilizer?
questions.answer: Device (servo/relay type) keeping output voltage nearly constant despite mains variation. Used for refrigerators, ACs, lab equipment.

questions.question: Per-Unit (p.u.) System?
questions.formula: p.u. = Actual / Base. Normalized representation; simplifies calculations across different voltage levels.

questions.question: Short Circuit Capacity (SCC) / Fault Level?
questions.formula: Fault MVA = Base MVA / p.u. impedance at fault. Used to select CB interrupting capacity.

questions.question: Diversity Factor & its benefit?
questions.formula: DF = Σ Individual Max Demands / Group Max Demand ≥ 1. Higher DF → smaller required plant capacity → cost saving.

questions.question: Copper vs Aluminium Cables — when to choose?
questions.answer: Copper: higher conductivity, smaller size, better joints; costly; heavier. Aluminium: lighter, cheaper; larger cross-section for same ampacity; common in distribution.

questions.question: What is Cable Derating?
questions.answer: Reduction of allowable current due to installation conditions (ambient temp, soil thermal resistivity, grouping, burial depth). Grouping multiple cables in one trench needs derating factors.

questions.question: XLPE vs PVC Insulation?
questions.answer: XLPE: higher temp rating (90 °C vs ~70 °C), lower dielectric loss, better current rating. PVC: cheaper, good mechanical strength, lower temp rating.

questions.question: Touch Voltage & Step Voltage (Earthing)?
questions.answer: Touch: voltage between energized object & ground potential at a person's hand/feet. Step: voltage between two feet 1 m apart during ground fault. Substation earthing design (grid + rods + crushed rock) limits both to safe levels.

questions.question: Why 3-Phase Power is preferred over Single-Phase in industry?
questions.answer: Constant power transfer, higher efficiency, smaller motor size, easier starting/torque, economical transmission.

questions.question: Star vs Delta Connection (Motors) — Practical use?
questions.answer: Star: lower phase voltage → lower starting current (star–delta starters). Delta: rated running connection; higher torque.

questions.question: No-Load Current in Induction Motor — why high?
questions.answer: Even at no-load, IM draws 20–40% rated current to establish magnetizing flux and supply core losses.

questions.question: Slip vs Torque in IM (Torque–Slip curve)?
questions.answer: Torque ∝ slip at low slip (linear region). Max torque (pull-out) at slip s = R2/X2. Beyond that, torque falls as slip increases (unstable region).

questions.question: Methods of Speed Control in Induction Motor?
questions.answer: 1) Stator voltage control (fan/pump). 2) V/f control (VFD — best). 3) Rotor resistance (slip-ring). 4) Pole changing (Dahlander). VFD keeps air-gap flux constant (constant V/f).
questions.aye_note: Directly relevant to AYE TechEngine PLC + VFD training tracks.

questions.question: Synchronous Condenser — what & why?
questions.answer: An over-excited synchronous motor running no mechanical load to supply reactive power (VARs) for PF correction and voltage support in grids.

questions.question: Why Transformer rating is in kVA, not kW?
questions.answer: Losses depend on voltage & current (not PF). Hence rating based on apparent power (kVA).

questions.question: Vector Group of Transformer (e.g., Dyn11)?
questions.answer: Shows winding connection & phase displacement. D=Delta, y=star, n=neutral. 11 indicates LV leads HV by 30°. Important for parallel operation and harmonics.

questions.question: Inrush Current in Transformers — causes & mitigation?
questions.answer: Cause: core residual flux + switching at voltage zero → high magnetizing surge. Mitigation: pre-insertion resistors, controlled switching at appropriate angle, inrush relays.

questions.question: CT Secondary — why must never be open?
questions.answer: Open circuit → high secondary voltage induced → dangerous to personnel & can damage CT insulation. Always short or load with burden.

questions.question: Battery Terms: C-rate, DoD, SoC?
questions.answer: C-rate: current relative to capacity (1C for 100 Ah = 100 A). DoD: % of capacity withdrawn. SoC: remaining charge %. Life increases when DoD kept moderate (80% vs 100%).

questions.question: Lead-acid vs Li-ion?
questions.answer: Lead-acid: cheap, heavy, lower energy density, tolerant to abuse, used in UPS/automotive. Li-ion: high energy density, light, better cycle life, needs BMS for safety; used in EVs, portable devices.

questions.question: What is BMS (Battery Management System)?
questions.answer: Electronics that monitor and protect cells: voltage, current, temperature, SoC/SoH estimation, cell balancing, fault isolation.

questions.question: Power Factor Correction Methods (Plant level)?
questions.answer: Static capacitors, APFC panels, synchronous condenser, STATCOM. Benefits: reduced kVA demand, lower losses, improved voltage profile.

questions.question: Soft Starter vs VFD (Induction Motors)?
questions.answer: Soft Starter: controls voltage during start → limits inrush; no speed control at steady state. VFD: controls frequency & voltage (V/f) → smooth start + full speed control + energy saving on variable-torque loads.

questions.question: What is an ideal transformer?
questions.answer: Hypothetical transformer with 100% efficiency. Assumptions: no copper loss, no iron loss, perfect magnetic coupling (Φ leakage = 0), infinite core permeability. Real transformers ≠ ideal, but efficiency is very high (95–99%).

questions.question: Efficiency of transformer (detailed)?
questions.formula: η = Output/Input × 100 = (V2 I2 cos φ2) / (V2 I2 cos φ2 + Pcore + Pcu) × 100. Depends on copper loss (I²R) & iron loss (hysteresis + eddy currents).

questions.question: Why transformer efficiency is high?
questions.answer: No moving parts → no mechanical losses. Only core & copper losses present. Proper design → efficiency > 95%.

questions.question: Condition for maximum efficiency of a transformer?
questions.answer: Maximum efficiency when Pcore = Pcu (Iron loss = Copper loss).

questions.question: Difference between core type and shell type transformer?
questions.answer: Core type: windings around two limbs, single flux path, better cooling, used in high voltage. Shell type: windings around central limb, double flux path, less cooling, used in low/medium voltage.

questions.question: What is all-day efficiency?
questions.answer: Used in distribution transformer. Ratio of kWh output / kWh input in 24 hours. Depends more on iron loss (since load varies).

questions.question: Why distribution transformer designed for low iron loss?
questions.answer: It works 24×7, mostly on light load. So iron loss (constant) dominates → efficiency maintained if iron loss reduced.

questions.question: What are harmonics in transformer?
questions.answer: Distortion in flux/current due to nonlinear magnetization. Causes: saturation, poor design, load nonlinearity. Effects: overheating, vibration, noise, reduced efficiency.

questions.question: What are autotransformers?
questions.answer: Single winding used as both primary & secondary. Advantages: smaller, cheaper, efficient. Disadvantages: no isolation between HV & LV.

questions.question: Difference between 2-winding transformer and autotransformer?
questions.answer: 2-winding: primary & secondary isolated; safer for HV. Autotransformer: common winding → smaller size; economical for small ratio (e.g., 220 V → 110 V).

questions.question: Open circuit test (OC test) of transformer?
questions.answer: Performed on LV side, HV kept open. Measures iron/core loss.

questions.question: Short circuit test (SC test) of transformer?
questions.answer: Performed on HV side (LV shorted). Measures copper loss.

questions.question: Why transformer rating in kVA not in kW?
questions.answer: Losses depend on voltage & current (V & A). Power factor doesn't affect losses. Hence transformer designed in kVA.

questions.question: What is tap changer in transformer?
questions.answer: Device to regulate secondary voltage. Types: On-load tap changer (OLTC) & Off-load tap changer.

questions.question: What is Buchholz relay?
questions.answer: Gas-operated protection device used in oil-immersed transformers. Detects faults like insulation failure, overheating, arcing.

questions.question: Cooling methods of transformer?
questions.answer: Air cooled: dry type, natural or forced air. Oil cooled: ONAN, ONAF, OFAF, OFWF.

questions.question: Transformer polarity test?
questions.answer: Checks relative polarity of windings. Ensures correct connection in parallel operation.

questions.question: Why transformers are operated in parallel?
questions.answer: 1) Load sharing. 2) Reliability — if one fails, others carry load. 3) Expansion possible.

questions.question: Conditions for parallel operation of transformers?
questions.answer: 1) Same voltage ratio. 2) Same polarity. 3) Similar % impedance. 4) Same phase sequence.

questions.question: What is leakage flux in transformer?
questions.answer: Part of flux which does not link with both windings. Causes leakage reactance → voltage drop & poor regulation.

questions.question: What is eddy current loss?
questions.answer: Loss due to induced circulating currents in core. Reduced by laminating the core.

questions.question: What is hysteresis loss?
questions.answer: Energy lost in magnetizing/demagnetizing core. Depends on material & frequency. Reduced by using CRGO steel.

questions.question: What is per-unit (p.u.) system?
questions.answer: Expressing electrical values (V, I, Z, P) as a fraction of base value. Simplifies calculations in power systems.

questions.question: Function of tap changing transformer?
questions.answer: To maintain constant secondary voltage under load variations. OLTC widely used in transmission systems.

questions.question: What is an Earth Fault?
questions.answer: Occurs when a phase conductor touches earth or earthed metal. Causes high current → damage, shock hazard. Protection: earth leakage relay, ELCB/RCCB. Example: broken insulation on cable.

questions.question: What is a Line-to-Line Fault?
questions.answer: Fault between two phase conductors. Causes unbalanced currents. Protection: overcurrent relay, distance relay in transmission.

questions.question: What is a Three-Phase Fault?
questions.answer: Fault involving all three phases simultaneously. Most severe fault → maximum current. Protection: circuit breaker + relay coordination.

questions.question: What is Sequence Components in Power System?
questions.answer: Decompose unbalanced 3-phase system into Positive, Negative, Zero sequence components. Helps fault analysis.

questions.question: What is a Relay in Power System?
questions.answer: Device that detects abnormal conditions (overcurrent, voltage, frequency, faults) and trips CB. Essential for system protection.

questions.question: What is an Overcurrent Relay? (detailed)
questions.answer: Operates when current exceeds preset value. Can be instantaneous or time-delay. Used in feeders and transformers.

questions.question: What is Differential Relay? (detailed)
questions.answer: Compares currents at two ends of equipment. Trips if difference > threshold → indicates internal fault. Example: transformer, generator protection.

questions.question: What is Distance Relay? (detailed)
questions.answer: Operates based on impedance measurement to fault. Used in long transmission lines. Can be impedance, reactance, or mho type.

questions.question: What is a Buchholz Relay? (detailed)
questions.answer: Gas-operated relay in oil-filled transformers. Detects internal faults like winding fault, overheating, arcing. Often installed between tank & conservator.

questions.question: Function of Lightning Arrester?
questions.answer: Protects electrical equipment from lightning surges. Diverts surge safely to earth. Types: rod-gap, horn-gap, valve type.

questions.question: What is Corona in Transmission Lines?
questions.answer: Ionization of air around conductor → hissing sound, ozone, energy loss. Occurs at high voltage. Mitigation: bundled conductors, increase conductor diameter.

questions.question: What is Skin Effect in AC?
questions.answer: AC current flows mostly on surface of conductor. Causes increase in effective resistance at high frequency. Reduced by stranded conductors (Litz wire).

questions.question: What is Proximity Effect? (detailed)
questions.answer: Non-uniform current distribution in a conductor due to magnetic field of nearby conductors. Causes extra losses & heating.

questions.question: What is Transmission Line Sag?
questions.answer: Vertical distance between conductor and straight line joining supports. Depends on conductor weight, span length, tension, temperature. Important for mechanical design & clearance.

questions.question: What is String Efficiency in Insulator String?
questions.formula: %Efficiency = (Voltage across string / (n × Avg unit voltage)) × 100. Normally <100% due to uneven voltage distribution. Improved by grading rings.

questions.question: What is Dielectric Loss?
questions.answer: Energy lost in insulation material when AC applied. Causes heating in cables & transformers.

questions.question: What is Power Factor? (detailed)
questions.formula: PF = cos φ = P/S. Indicates efficiency of electrical system. Lagging = inductive load; Leading = capacitive load.

questions.question: Why Improve Power Factor?
questions.answer: Reduces line losses; reduces kVA demand; improves voltage profile.

questions.question: Methods to Improve Power Factor?
questions.answer: Capacitor banks; synchronous condensers; proper load management.

questions.question: (Continuation of PF improvement)
questions.answer: Combine APFC panels with capacitor banks for automatic correction in industrial plants.

questions.question: What is Stepper Motor?
questions.answer: Converts electrical pulses → mechanical rotation. Used in robotics, CNC, printers.

questions.question: What is Servo Motor?
questions.answer: DC/AC motor with feedback loop to control position/speed. High precision → robotics & automation.

questions.question: Difference between Stepper & Servo Motor?
questions.answer: Stepper: open-loop, high discrete-step precision, moderate torque (3D printer, CNC). Servo: closed-loop, high continuous precision, high torque (robotics, conveyors).

questions.question: What is a DC Motor? (detailed)
questions.answer: Converts electrical energy → mechanical energy using DC supply. Types: Shunt, Series, Compound. Applications: elevators, cranes, rolling mills.

questions.question: Difference between DC Shunt & Series Motor?
questions.answer: Shunt: field winding parallel, nearly constant speed, moderate torque, fans/lathes. Series: field winding series, speed varies with load, high starting torque, cranes/traction.

questions.question: What is DC Compound Motor?
questions.answer: Combines shunt + series windings. Good starting torque + relatively constant speed.

questions.question: What is Back EMF in DC Motor?
questions.formula: Eb = V − Ia·Ra. Generated by rotor cutting magnetic flux. Opposes applied voltage → limits armature current.

questions.question: Why DC Motor Speed decreases with Load?
questions.answer: As load increases → armature current ↑ → voltage drop ↑ → Back EMF ↓ → speed slightly drops.

questions.question: What is an AC Induction Motor?
questions.answer: Rotating magnetic field induces current in rotor → rotor rotates. Types: Squirrel Cage, Slip Ring. Applications: pumps, fans, compressors.

questions.question: What is Slip in Induction Motor?
questions.formula: s = ((Ns − Nr) / Ns) × 100%. Small (0.5–5%) for normal operation.

questions.question: Difference between Synchronous & Induction Motor?
questions.answer: Synchronous: constant speed, permanent-magnet or DC rotor, leading/lagging PF, large generators. Induction: slightly less than synchronous speed, shorted or wound rotor, lagging PF, fans/pumps.

questions.question: What is a Transformer? (concise)
questions.answer: AC device → changes voltage levels without changing frequency. Principle: mutual induction.

questions.question: Transformer Rating in kVA?
questions.answer: Rating depends on voltage & current, not power factor. P = VI cos φ → kVA = V × I.

questions.question: Difference between Core & Shell Type Transformer?
questions.answer: Core: windings around core limbs, simple flux path, cheaper. Shell: windings around central limb, double flux path, expensive.

questions.question: What is No-Load Current of Transformer?
questions.answer: Current drawn by primary at no-load. Supplies core loss + magnetizing current.

questions.question: What is Short Circuit Impedance of Transformer?
questions.answer: Voltage required to produce rated current in shorted secondary. Determines fault level & voltage drop.

questions.question: What is OLTC (On-Load Tap Changer)?
questions.answer: Allows voltage adjustment without interrupting load. Maintains secondary voltage constant.

questions.question: What is Overload Capacity of Transformer?
questions.answer: % above rated load it can carry for short duration without damage. Typical: 10–20% for 1–2 hours.

questions.question: What is a Circuit Breaker? (recap)
questions.answer: Switch that automatically interrupts fault current. Types: Air, Oil, SF6, Vacuum.

questions.question: What is Rating of CB?
questions.answer: Rated voltage, current, interrupting capacity, frequency.

questions.question: Difference between MCB & MCCB?
questions.answer: MCB: up to 100 A, thermal/magnetic trip, domestic. MCCB: 100–2500 A, thermal/magnetic/electronic trip, industrial.

questions.question: What is Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (ELCB)?
questions.answer: Detects current leakage to earth → trips. Protects humans from shock & fires.

questions.question: What is Relay? (recap)
questions.answer: Protective device → senses abnormal condition → trips CB. Types: overcurrent, differential, distance.

questions.question: What is HRC Fuse?
questions.answer: High Rupturing Capacity fuse. Safely interrupts high fault currents. Used in transformers, panels.

questions.question: Difference between Fuse & CB?
questions.answer: Fuse: not reusable, melts wire, fixed current limit. CB: reusable, mechanical/electronic trip, adjustable.

questions.question: What is a Voltage Stabilizer? (recap)
questions.answer: Maintains output voltage constant despite input variation. Types: relay, servo, electronic.

questions.question: What is UPS?
questions.answer: Uninterruptible Power Supply. Provides backup DC/AC power → maintains continuous operation.

questions.question: Single-Phase vs Three-Phase Supply?
questions.answer: Single-phase: 230 V, higher current for same power, residential. Three-phase: 3×230/400 V, lower current, industrial & transmission.
